petitioner_background: The petitioner is a lawyer who works as a public prosecutor and specialist in institutional policy and technology innovation. He completed his graduate legal education earning a master of laws (LLM) and doctor of juridical science (SJD) degrees, and has spent most of his professional career as a public prosecutor where he has led several innovation initiatives. He seeks classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A, INA section 203(b)(1)(A).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not specify the particular evidence submitted, but the Director concluded that the Petitioner met the criterion relating to judging the work of others.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that the Petitioner satisfied this criterion at the initial evidentiary stage. The AAO did not disturb this finding, noting that the Director has already determined that the Petitioner met at least three criteria.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not specify the particular scholarly articles submitted, but the Director concluded that the Petitioner met the criterion relating to authorship of scholarly articles in the field.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that the Petitioner satisfied this criterion at the initial evidentiary stage. The AAO did not disturb this finding.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The decision does not specify the particular evidence, but references the Petitioner's work as a public prosecutor where he led several innovation initiatives. The Director concluded the Petitioner performed in a leading or critical role for an establishment with a distinguished reputation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that the Petitioner satisfied this criterion at the initial evidentiary stage. The AAO did not disturb this finding.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted evidence relating to awards as one of six criteria claimed. The specific awards are not detailed in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the Director's final merits discussion did not meaningfully address the evidence relating to the Petitioner's awards. The Director did not consider whether this evidence, individually or collectively with other evidence, showed sustained national or international acclaim or demonstrated that the Petitioner is among the small percentage at the very top of the field. On appeal, the Petitioner argued the Director did not adequately explain why the submitted evidence was deemed insufficient to meet this criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i), and the AAO agreed the evidence must be reconsidered on remand.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted evidence relating to published materials about him as one of six claimed criteria. The specific publications are not detailed in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the Director's final merits discussion did not meaningfully address the evidence relating to published materials about the Petitioner. The Director failed to evaluate whether this evidence, individually or collectively, shows sustained national or international acclaim. The AAO instructed that on remand the Director should properly consider this evidence as part of the totality of the record.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted evidence relating to contributions to his field as one of six claimed criteria. The specific contributions are not detailed in the AAO decision, though they relate to his work in institutional policy and technology innovation as a prosecutor.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the Director's final merits discussion did not meaningfully address the evidence relating to the Petitioner's contributions to his field. The Petitioner argued on appeal that the Director did not adequately explain why the submitted evidence was deemed insufficient to meet the criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(v), and the AAO agreed this evidence must be reconsidered on remand as part of the totality of the record.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter for entry of a new decision. The AAO found that the Director's final merits analysis did not consider the record in its entirety and lacked a detailed discussion of the evidence. Although the Petitioner submitted evidence relating to six criteria, the final merits discussion only briefly addressed evidence related to three criteria, and it was unclear whether the Director considered the Petitioner's detailed response to a request for evidence since that evidence was not referenced in the decision. Because the decision was based on an incomplete review of the record, the Petitioner was not provided with a sufficient explanation of the reasons for denial as a basis for his appeal. On remand, the Director must conduct a final merits analysis of the totality of the record, including evidence submitted for all claimed criteria.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: The AAO found that the Director's decision was based on an incomplete review of the record. The Director's final merits analysis failed to consider the record in its entirety, lacked a detailed discussion of the evidence, and only briefly addressed three of the six criteria for which evidence was submitted. It was unclear whether the Director considered the Petitioner's detailed response to a request for evidence, as that response was not referenced in the decision. The AAO held that under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(i), the Director must explain in writing the specific reasons for denial sufficient to allow the Petitioner a fair opportunity to contest the decision and allow meaningful appellate review, which did not occur here.
